text: Machine learning teaches computers to learn from data without being explicitly programmed for every task. In supervised learning, the computer is given a set of training data that includes input-output pairs. For example, you could show it emails labeled "spam" or "not spam." Using this data, the computer builds a model—a mathematical pattern that captures the relationship between inputs and outputs. Once the model is ready, it can make predictions on new, unseen data. The accuracy of those predictions measures how often the model is correct. The more high-quality training data available, the more accurate the model usually becomes.
